name: Snowboarding
description: Snowboarding history, tricks, athletes, and competitions

questions.q: A snowboard half-pipe is built in the shape of which letter?

source.notes: A long trough with curved walls used for aerial tricks.

questions.q: In which year did snowboarding make its debut as an official Olympic sport?

source.notes: Debuted at the Nagano 1998 Winter Olympics.

questions.q: Snowboarding's Winter Olympic debut happened in which country?

source.notes: Nagano, Japan hosted the 1998 Winter Games.

questions.q: Who is widely credited as the inventor of the modern snowboard and founder of Burton Snowboards?

source.notes: Jake Burton Carpenter founded Burton in 1977 in Vermont.

questions.q: Since 2002, the Winter X Games have been held annually in which Colorado mountain town?

source.notes: Moved to Aspen's Buttermilk Mountain in 2002.

questions.q: Red-haired American legend Shaun White holds the record for most gold medals in which extreme sport competition?

source.notes: Nicknamed 'The Flying Tomato' and later 'The Animal'.

questions.q: A snowboarding trick described as a '360' involves how much rotation?

source.notes: 360° = one complete rotation; 540°, 720°, 900°, 1080° follow the same pattern.

questions.q: Ross Rebagliati became the first-ever Olympic gold medalist in snowboarding in 1998 while competing for which country?

source.notes: Won the giant slalom at Nagano 1998.

questions.q: Ross Rebagliati briefly had his 1998 gold medal stripped after testing positive for which substance before it was reinstated?

source.notes: THC was not on the IOC banned list at the time; medal was restored.

questions.q: Competitive snowboarding's international governing body is known by which three-letter abbreviation?

source.notes: Fédération Internationale de Ski et de Snowboard.

questions.q: What was Shaun White's well-known early-career nickname, inspired by his long red hair?

source.notes: He later cut his hair and reportedly grew tired of the nickname.

questions.q: The edges of a snowboard are typically reinforced with what material for grip on icy snow?

source.notes: Usually steel; allows the rider to carve turns.

questions.q: Which snowboarding discipline, added to the Olympic program in 2014, sends riders down a course with rails and jumps scored on overall style?

source.notes: Added at Sochi 2014.

questions.q: Which Australian snowboarder won Olympic gold in women's half-pipe at the 2010 Vancouver Games?

source.notes: Bright also won silver in the half-pipe in 2014.

questions.q: Which snowboarding discipline involves riding off-piste in untouched powder, rather than on groomed runs or in parks?

source.notes: Emphasizes natural terrain; popularized by riders like Craig Kelly.

questions.q: In parallel giant slalom snowboarding, how many competitors race simultaneously on adjacent courses?

source.notes: Head-to-head bracket format; fastest rider advances.

questions.q: The Big Air snowboarding event is based around which core element?

source.notes: A large single kicker; riders perform one trick per run.

questions.q: Burton Snowboards, one of the sport's most iconic brands, was founded in which U.S. state?

source.notes: Founded in Londonderry, VT in 1977 and later headquartered in Burlington, VT.

questions.q: Which Shaun White event produced his three Olympic gold medals in 2006, 2010, and 2018?

source.notes: Won men's half-pipe gold at Turin 2006, Vancouver 2010, and PyeongChang 2018.

questions.q: Women's snowboard cross was added to the Winter Olympic program in which year?

source.notes: Debuted at Turin 2006.

questions.q: In what year was the first FIS Snowboard World Championships held?

source.notes: Held in Lienz, Austria.

questions.q: Who is considered the 'Godfather of Freeriding', a snowboarder whose big-mountain influence shaped modern backcountry style?

source.notes: Four-time World Champion in the 1980s–90s; died in a 2003 avalanche.
